item.title: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
As discovered by C-Dives,

"bloody.png" is NC, "deform.jpg" is ND.
https://www.flickr.com/photos/antonolsen/1225022737/
https://www.flickr.com/photos/sanfranphotos/1361862200/

As neither license is compliant with CC BY-SA 3.0, they have since been removed

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Decided to respond because this comment's been bothering me for awhile.

Starts out very strong, but by the time we get to the test logs it basically just seems to turn into grossness for its own sake.

That's the point. Bathrooms are often seen as a place of purity. People abhor unclean and fetid locations of places that they associate with cleanliness because it violates every notion of how they use the bathroom in their privacy.

All I did was take this up to eleven. It's served the plot and it is the main anomalous feature; I'd understand if you felt that the main anomalous feature was boring, but it's absurd to downvote something based on what it can accomplish in the feelings of the reader. I'd say I did a pretty damn good job of grossing the ever-living shit out of people.

This ends up just being a "D-class guy gets thrown into an awful situation for no particular reason" skip, and I really feel like it could be so much more than that.

Literally all D-Class personnel are usually thrown in awful situations because they're expendable manforce. How else would the Foundation test the capabilities of an anomaly?

I don't think you understand that this is how the Foundation operates. They contain and analyze SCPs for the sake of trying to make sense of the abnormal; it wouldn't really be the Foundation if they didn't chuck a D-Class at it to see what it does to a human, now would it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nah.

I like the concept here a lot, and I've read the other comments on this discussion page, but the framing here is just not doing it for me. Starts out very strong, but by the time we get to the test logs it basically just seems to turn into grossness for its own sake. I'm aware of the attempt at metaphor here but it just doesn't come across at all. This ends up just being a "D-class guy gets thrown into an awful situation for no particular reason" skip, and I really feel like it could be so much more than that.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ultimately, this is a no vote for me.

I've given my thoughts in the SCPD server but I figured I'd go ahead and outline them here.

+ Show crit
- Hide crit

I feel the reason why he became stuck is not based on any sort of established internal logic, as far as I can tell. When it comes to storytelling, it does not feel rewarding as a reader/viewer to have the established rules of the story broken when there is not a reason given for it. In this case, the rolling back the paper undoing the deterioration of the bathroom. It works once and then it doesn't work the second time. Why is this?

I understand the implication here is supposed to be creepy. And I'm not saying the reason has to be spelled out exactly but there's not really even a hint of a reason.

You then told me that the foundation is supposed to be a metaphor for peer pressure and. as you described to me, this is intended overall to be a metaphor for "routines, rituals, habits, and addictions." I truthfully believe this is weak in the framing of this story.

First of all, the Foundation "commanding" the D-Class to continue I do not believe is a very strong metaphor for Peer Pressure. Peer Pressure is a far more insidious thing that is generally much more subconscious to the victim. The common portrayal of it in fiction is, overall, pretty incorrect and more stereotypical than indicative of what people actually go through.

Second of all, I feel this whole thing would have worked far better as a metaphor if you had focused in on drug use, as you had mentioned at the start of our previous discussion. Drug Addiction is a scary as shit thing and if you had made this a strong parallel to it, I believe the entire thing would have been more impactful.

I believe a fairly simple thing to do would be making the use of the paper towel dispenser cause actual physical pleasure to the user. This could compel the user to keep using it past the point of when he really should stop doing so. And once he gets stuck and the room continues deteriorating into hell, the pulling of the paper becomes his only source of actual pleasure until the effects of it dull to the point of barely even working.

This, to me, would've made for a stronger metaphor for drug addiction.

Anyway, ultimately I am no-voting it because I actually think this is generally well written and I think the concept itself has merit and potential. I just think the execution could use some work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There are paper towel dispensers that have these, like, indented nobs on the side that you can spin to force the paper out and I… think they work in reverse as well? You can see one on the side of this dispenser.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was actually really, really good. +1, first off.

But I do wonder, was the D-Class being influenced by the room or did he just go insane because of the isolation?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dipped my hand in the shit. Maybe I'll die faster if it gets in my nervous system.

unsanitary

1+

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I remember critiquing this in its draft form and I loved it then. Now that everything I brought up has been fixed, I love it even more.

Upvoted. Well done!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
do not appear to have any other function other than to dry human hands.

This sounds weirdly specific to me.

During cleanup, one of the creatures had a message carved into its abdomen with a small, sharp piece of a ceramic tile.

The way this is phrased I thought this happened during cleanup. As if one of the staff carved the message. I also don't see how you'd carve that message into any of the creatures described. Maybe make it something like they found the message smeared on the remains of the mirror / walls?

Overall I really enjoyed this though. Delightfully disgusting! :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh. Yeah, that might be the case here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Actually in hindsight, I probably should've specified how he did it. Will make the edit later on.

… I think there might be some fundamental difference in how paper towel dispensers work where I'm from and where you're from.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It wasn't already closed off?!

Well, they were doing an experiment and they had a bunch of guards outside the door.

I'm having a hard time picturing how you could stuff paper towels back into the dispenser after pulling them out, too.

Well, you can just take the cover off and start the reversing the roll.

Actually in hindsight, I probably should've specified how he did it. Will make the edit later on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
After losing contact with the subject, it was presumed that D-22222 perished inside the room. With no way to access the restroom, it was proposed to close off the restroom from the public until further notice.

It wasn't already closed off?!

This is pretty good but there are just a few too many small issues that stop me from upvoting. I'm having a hard time picturing how you could stuff paper towels back into the dispenser after pulling them out, too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yup. And it only took like, five articles to make it happen.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Say what you want about this skip; you can write a pretty damn good exploration log.

+1

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
You know nothing. Hell is only a word. The reality is much, much worse.

- Dr. Weir, Event Horizon

Thanks to Elogee FishTruck, KindlyTurtleClem, and DrCaroll for their critique.

Image Credits:

Fig 1.1: https://pixabay.com/en/hand-dryers-paper-dispenser-toilet-100786/
Fig 1.2: https://www.flickr.com/photos/antonolsen/1225022737/in/photostream/
Fig 1.3: https://www.flickr.com/photos/sanfranphotos/1361862200/
